sections.title: Java Basics & Syntax
OOP Concepts
Data Types & Variables
sections.content:
## Understanding Data Types
Java supports two main categories of data types: primitive types and reference types. Primitive types are the basic building blocks, while reference types refer to objects.

### The Eight Primitive Data Types
Java provides exactly eight primitive data types that are built into the language. The byte type uses one byte and stores values from -128 to 127. The short type uses two bytes and stores larger values than byte. The int type is the most commonly used integer type and uses four bytes. The long type uses eight bytes for very large numbers and requires an 'L' suffix. The float type uses four bytes for decimal numbers and requires an 'f' suffix. The double type uses eight bytes and provides more precision for decimal numbers. The boolean type can only be true or false. The char type uses two bytes and stores single characters.

### Variable Scope and Lifetime
Static variables are shared across all instances of a class and maintain their value throughout the program. Instance variables belong to individual objects and each object has its own copy. Local variables are declared inside methods and can only be used within that method. Final variables cannot be changed once assigned and typically represent constants.

### Type Casting
Implicit casting, also known as widening, happens automatically when converting from a smaller data type to a larger one. For example, an integer can be automatically converted to a long. Explicit casting, also known as narrowing, requires manual intervention when converting from a larger type to a smaller one. You must specify the target type in parentheses.

### Working with Strings
Strings have many useful methods for manipulation. The length method returns how many characters are in the string. The charAt method gets a character at a specific position. The substring method extracts a portion of the string. The toUpperCase and toLowerCase methods change the case. The equals method compares strings for equality. The indexOf and contains methods search for characters or substrings. The split method breaks a string into an array based on a delimiter. The replace method substitutes characters or substrings.

### StringBuilder for Efficient String Manipulation
StringBuilder is a mutable alternative to String that is more efficient when you need to make many modifications. The append method adds content to the end. The insert method adds content at a specific position. The reverse method reverses the entire string. The toString method converts the StringBuilder back into a regular String.

sections.title: Control Flow
Arrays & Strings
sections.content:
## Working with Arrays
Arrays are containers that hold multiple values of the same data type. They provide an efficient way to store and access related data. Arrays have a fixed size that is determined when they are created.

### Creating and Using Arrays
You can declare an array by specifying the data type followed by square brackets. You can initialize an array with a specific size or provide initial values using curly braces. Once created, you access array elements using their index, which starts at zero. Arrays have a length property that tells you how many elements they contain.

```java
int[] arr = new int[5];        // Array of size 5
int[] arr = {1, 2, 3};         // Initialize with values
int value = arr[0];            // Access first element
int len = arr.length;          // Get length
```

### Two-Dimensional Arrays
Two-dimensional arrays, or matrices, are arrays of arrays. They are useful for representing tables of data or grids. You can access elements by providing two indices, one for the row and one for the column. Nested loops are commonly used to iterate through all elements.

```java
int[][] matrix = new int[3][3];
int[][] matrix = {{1, 2}, {3, 4}};

for (int i = 0; i < matrix.length; i++) {
    for (int j = 0; j < matrix[i].length; j++) {
        System.out.println(matrix[i][j]);
    }
}
```

### Array Utilities
The Arrays utility class provides helpful methods for working with arrays. You can sort arrays using the sort method, which arranges elements in ascending order. Binary search finds an element quickly in a sorted array. The fill method sets all elements to a specific value. Copy methods allow you to duplicate entire arrays or portions of them. The equals method compares two arrays for equality.

## Working with Strings
Strings are sequences of characters and are one of the most frequently used data types. Strings are immutable in Java, meaning they cannot be changed after creation.

### Creating Strings
You can create a string using a string literal, which is stored in a special memory area called the string pool. You can also create a new String object using the new keyword, which creates a separate object in memory. The valueOf method converts other data types into strings.

```java
String str = "Hello";  // Literal
String str = new String("Hello");  // Object
```

### Essential String Methods
The length method returns the number of characters. The charAt method returns the character at a specific position. The substring method extracts a portion of the string. The toUpperCase and toLowerCase methods change the case of all characters. The equals method checks if two strings have the same content. The contains method checks if a string includes a specific substring. The indexOf method finds the position of a character or substring. The split method divides a string into an array based on a delimiter.
